item.title: common lisp utils
item.description: a collection of utility functions for common lisp arrays ;; (defun array-width (2d-array) ;; (car (cdr (array-dimensions 2d-array)))) ;; (defun array-height (2d-array) ;; (car (array-dimensions 2d-array))) (defun array-rows (arr) "number of rows in array" (elt (array-dimensions arr) (- (length (array-dimensions arr)) 2))) (defun array-cols (arr) "number of columns in array" (elt (array-dimensions arr) (- (length (array-dimensions arr)) 1))) (defun array-depth (arr) "depth of a tensor of the third order" (elt (array-dimensions arr) (- (length (array-dimensions arr)) 3))) (defun array-size (arr) (array-total-size arr)) turn multidimensional (nested) list into array: (defun list-dimensions (list) (if (and (eq (type-of list) 'cons) (eq (type-of (car list)) 'cons)) (append (list (length list)) (list-dimensions (car list))) (list (length list)))) (defun list->array (list) (make-array (list-dimensions list) :initial-contents list)) turn multidimensional array into a list: (defun array->list (array) (let* ((dimensions (array-dimensions array)) (depth (1- (length dimensions))) (indices (make-list (1+ depth) :initial-element 0))) (labels ((recurse (n) (loop for j below (nth n dimensions) do (setf (nth n indices) j) collect (if (= n depth) (apply #'aref array indices) (recurse (1+ n)))))) (recurse 0)))) to generate an array with random elements of arbitrary dimensions: (defun random-float (min max) (+ min (* (random 1.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 2
item.description: given the following game tree original tree image from homework: implement pruning, show the optimal tree and the pruned subtrees (explain) a step by step execution, at each step the modified node and the nodes that it depends on to determine its value are highlighted red, the assumption here is that the depth-first search algorithm prefers the left side (applies recursion to left node first, then right node), next to each node written are the values of for the state associated with it, the pruned nodes are colored blue.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 3
item.description: scroll to the end if you’re only interested in the solution and the steps in raw text.. homework on constraint satisfaction problem using common lisp graphs graph image from homework: gotta solve this as an rgba map coloring problem make use of forward checking prioritize vertices with least constraining (least edges, least constraints) to most constraining (most edges), in case of a draw, use the variable with the most remaining colors (least constrained), if there is another tie, choose the vertex to the left first we define the graph: (defparameter *mygraph* (make-graph :adjacency-list '((a .

item.title: common lisp
item.description: for loop (loop for x in '(1 2 3) collect x) ;; returns list (loop for x in '(1 2 3) do (print x)) ;; prints the numbers functions (defun <name> (list of arguments) "docstring" (body)) plist defining a plist (setf my-plist '(alt "yes" bar "no")) retrieve a value by key (getf my-plist 'alt) change the value that corresponds to a key (setf (getf my-plist 'alt) 45) get a list of the attributes (loop for (key value) on my-plist by #'cddr collect key) unbind variable (makunbound 'foo) undefine funciton (fmakunbound 'function) quicklisp need to install it manually afaik curl -O https://beta.

item.title: common lisp graphs
item.description: working with graphs in common lisp my initial approach for simplicity ill represent a graph as a list of cons’s, if we are dealing with a weighted graph, we use the second item in the cons as a cons with the first item being the node and the second being the weight i implemented the helper functions node-weight and node for this purpose an edge is written/stored in the format '(<src> .

item.title: common lisp hunchentoot
item.description: for reference https://lispcookbook.github.io/cl-cookbook/web.html hunchentoot is a common lisp library for creating webservers begin by loading the package using quicklisp: (ql:quickload :hunchentoot) a simple example: (defparameter *acceptor* (make-instance 'hunchentoot:easy-acceptor :port 4242)) (setf (hunchentoot:acceptor-document-root *acceptor*) ;; location of pages to server statically #p"path/to/www") (hunchentoot:start *acceptor*) ;; run the server to serve a dynamic file (one that is generated per-request) we define a handler: (hunchentoot:define-easy-handler (say-yo :uri "/yo") (name) (setf (hunchentoot:content-type*) "text/plain") (format nil "Hey~@[ ~A~]!

item.title: common lisp lparallel
item.description: lparallel is a multithreading library for common lisp, it is useful because the ANSI CL standard doesnt have multithreading it is available via quicklisp: (ql:quickload :lparallel) to get the number of cores we have to use the serapeum package: (ql:quickload :serapeum) usage: (serapeum:count-cpus) 16 in the context of lparallel, a kernel is an abstract entity that schedules and executes tasks. the lparallel kernel API is meant to describe parallelism in a generic manner.

item.title: constraint satisfaction problem
item.description: refer to [chapter 6] a constraint satisfaction problem consists of three components, , , and : is a set of variables, is a set of domains, , one for each variable is a set of constraints that specify allowable combinations of values a domain, , consists of a set of allowable values, , for variable . for example, a boolean variable would have the domain . different variables can have different domains of different sizes.

item.title: learning math functions with neural networks
item.description: see polynomial regression quick demo linear regression i could’ve used my code for multivariate linear regression but i wanna study how neural networks can do regression tasks if we were to try approximating a linear function using a simple multilayer perceptron we would see that since most activation functions squash the values of the network into a small range like in case of the sigmoid, it would be hard to regress to functions whose codomain is different that that of the activation functions, so we probably want to use a special activation function, or even easier, drop activation functions altogether, this way the neural network would basically be doing linear regression lets begin by trying to learn a linear function, for that we need to be able to generate random linear functions, perhaps with some noise, so we write a simple function for that: (defun generate-points-along-function (fn noise-variance n &optional (range 1000)) "generate n points along a function with some random noise.

item.title: maxima in lisp
item.description: i ended up giving up on using maxima as a visualization/symbolic math computation library for lisp, it wasnt made with this usecase in mind i guess, and so its an utter nightmare to lisp with it maxima is written in lisp and so it can be used as a modified/extended lisp runtime how to to start the lisp runtime use the command: rmaxima -r 'to_lisp();' can also run this in emacs’ slime/sly with (sly "rmaxima -r to_lisp();") alternatively, we can clone maxima’s source code to the subdirectory local-projects of the quicklisp installation directory (usually ~/quicklisp unless you’ve modified it), follow the instructions at https://sourceforge.

item.title: trees in common lisp
item.description: implementation of trees in common lisp see trees in lisp (defun tree-to-latex-forest (tree) "turn a tree into a runnable tikz block" (format t "\\begin{forest}") (tree-to-latex-forest-recurse tree) (format t "\\end{forest}")) (defun tree-to-latex-forest-recurse (tree) "helper function for tree-to-latex-forest-for-org" (if (not (eq tree nil)) (progn (format t "[~a,circle,draw" (car tree)) (loop for child in (cdr tree) do (tree-to-latex-forest-recurse child)) (format t "]")) (format t "[,phantom]")) tree) (let ((my-tree '(24 (12) (12 (1 nil (4 nil (5 nil (9 nil (10 nil nil))))) nil) (28 nil (45 nil (89 nil nil)))))) (tree-to-latex-forest my-tree))

item.title: movies
item.description: a small list of movies that have shaped my love for cinema, not that i even go to the cinema if you’re a normalfag, you may find most of these movies lean toward the obscure side and you probably wouldnt like them psychotraumatic movies that could fuck you up emotionally, be it by watching a mental illness take its toll on a character or by seeing them go through traumatic events these movies are very extreme, as there is alot of sadism, perversion and inhumanity (not all of them are this extreme though, e.
